coredata.dc:title: Room-temperature phosphorescent carbonized polymer dots as superior photoresponsive oxidase mimics for colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode enzyme-free sensing of uric acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Shiqing
Sun, Xiaojie
Zhang, Lifang
Miao, Yanming
Yan, Guiqin
coredata.dc:description: Photoresponsive nanozymes are widely used in analysis and other fields owing to their controllable catalysis and low cost, but most have relatively low catalytic efficiency. In this study, room-temperature phosphorescent carbonized polymer dots (PCPDs) were optimally prepared through a one-pot hydrothermal method using polyacrylic acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. Their photoresponsive oxidase-like activity was confirmed for the first time. The sub-fluorophore and highly-crosslinked structure endow PCPDs with excellent enzymatic properties. The PCPDs have strong affinity (K m = 0.1266 mM) and extremely high catalytic efficiency (V max = 2.0194 × 10−6 M s−1) over 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and the reaction is dozens of times faster compared with most photoresponsive oxidases (10−8 M s−1 level). Uric acid (UA) is an important biomarker, and its sensitive detection is particularly critical. The PCPDs were irradiated for 1 min to oxidize TMB into dark blue, and the strong reducing property of UA faded the dark blue and enhanced the fluorescence (FL) of the system. The colorimetric/FL dual-mode method established has detection limits over UA of 77.8 and 48.1 nM, respectively, and linear ranges of 0.6–55 and 0.6–60 μM, respectively. Smartphones were also used to identify dual-mode RGB to detect UA. Finally, the PCPDs were successfully used to detect UA in human serum, urine and saliva. This study presents a natural-enzyme-free UA sensing platform based on PCPDs, and more importantly, provides a new type of highly efficient photoresponsive oxidases with great potential for analytical sensing and other fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room-temperature phosphorescent carbonized polymer dot
Photoresponsive oxidase-like
Singlet oxygen
Colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode
Uric acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism on photoresponsive oxidase-like activity of PCPDs
Feasibility and mechanism of uric acid detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, B, C) TEM and HRTEM images of PCPDs; (D, E, F) XRD patterns, FT-IR spectra and XPS full spectra of PCPDs; (G, H, I) Fine spectra of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s of PCPDs; (J, K, L) Solid-state FL images, afterglow images, and RTP decay spectra of different PCPDs.

(A) UV–vis spectra of different reaction systems; (B–F) Effects of pH, PCPDs concentration, TMB concentration, temperature and illumination time on catalytic activity (Abs: 652 nm; FL (ex/em): 335 nm/408 nm); (G) Changes in catalytic activity of PCPDs at different storage time; (H) Continuous stepwise behavior of PCPDs oxidase-like activity when switching the light source on and off.

(A) Catalytic effect of PCPDs after ventilation of N2; (B) Effect of scavengers on catalytic activity of PCPDs; (C, D, E) EPR signals of TEMP-1O2 in water, DMPO-·O2 − in methanol and DMPO-·OH in water; (F) Photoresponsive oxidase-like activity mechanism of PCPDs; (G) Mechanism of UA detection.

(A, B) Changes in absorbance and FL with UA concentration; (C) Linear relationship between absorbance or FL and UA concentration; (D, F) RGB values of colorimetric/FL system and 4 × 4 colorimetric array; (E, G) Linear relationship between gray value of the colorimetric/FL system and UA concentration; (H) Selectivity of dual-mode UA sensing ([UA] = 20 μM, [Leu] = [Phe] = [Trp] = [Val] = [Glu] = [Urea] = 400 μM, [Cys] = [AA] = [GSH] = 2 μM); (I) Anti-interference of UA sensing system ([UA] = 50 μM, [Al3+] = [Ca2+] = [NO3 −] = [Cu2+] = [K+] = [Mg2+] = [Mn2+] = [Na+] = [NH4 +] = [Zn2+] = 800 μM, [Fe2+] = 4.8 μM, [Fe3+] = 48 μM).
Preparation of the PCPDs Photoresponsive Oxidase-like and Its Application for Colorimetric/Fluorescent Dual-Mode Method or Smartphone-Based Rapid Detection of UA.

head.ce:title: Room-temperature phosphorescent carbonized polymer dots as superior photoresponsive oxidase mimics for colorimetric-fluorescence dual-mode enzyme-free sensing of uric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 030000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Shanxi Normal University, Taiyuan, 030000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photoresponsive nanozymes are widely used in analysis and other fields owing to their controllable catalysis and low cost, but most have relatively low catalytic efficiency. In this study, room-temperature phosphorescent carbonized polymer dots (PCPDs) were optimally prepared through a one-pot hydrothermal method using polyacrylic acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. Their photoresponsive oxidase-like activity was confirmed for the first time. The sub-fluorophore and highly-crosslinked structure endow PCPDs with excellent enzymatic properties. The PCPDs have strong affinity (K m = 0.1266 mM) and extremely high catalytic efficiency (V max = 2.0194 × 10−6 M s−1) over 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and the reaction is dozens of times faster compared with most photoresponsive oxidases (10−8 M s−1 level). Uric acid (UA) is an important biomarker, and its sensitive detection is particularly critical. The PCPDs were irradiated for 1 min to oxidize TMB into dark blue, and the strong reducing property of UA faded the dark blue and enhanced the fluorescence (FL) of the system. The colorimetric/FL dual-mode method established has detection limits over UA of 77.8 and 48.1 nM, respectively, and linear ranges of 0.6–55 and 0.6–60 μM, respectively. Smartphones were also used to identify dual-mode RGB to detect UA. Finally, the PCPDs were successfully used to detect UA in human serum, urine and saliva. This study presents a natural-enzyme-free UA sensing platform based on PCPDs, and more importantly, provides a new type of highly efficient photoresponsive oxidases with great potential for analytical sensing and other fields.

Room-temperature phosphorescent carbonized polymer dots (PCPDs) as superior photoresponsive oxidase-like and application for colorimetric/fluorescent dual-mode method or smartphone-based rapid enzyme-free detection of uric acid (UA).
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Natural enzymes are easily inactivated under non-physiological conditions, and their high costs and harsh storage conditions limit their applications in many fields. These limitations have urged the development of new nanozymes (Du et al., 2018). Nanozymes have both nanomaterial properties and enzyme-like activity, and are outstanding with low cost, high stability, easy activity control, and easy large-scale preparation. They have been widely used in biosensing, environmental management, tumor treatment and other fields (Chen et al., 2022; Hang et al., 2024; Yao et al., 2022). Nanozymes have been used to successfully simulate the catalytic activities of peroxidase (POD), oxidase, and superoxide dismutase (SOD) (Du et al., 2018). The existing research is focused on POD-like enzymes, but their electron acceptor, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), decomposes easily and has certain biotoxicity (Lian et al., 2023). Moreover, adding additional oxidants complicates the reaction system and is not conducive to improving sensing performance. Oxidase-like enzymes that can catalyze molecular oxygen are gradually replacing POD-like enzymes in some applications (Wu et al., 2021).

Controlling the catalytic activity and processes of nanozymes is crucial for their advanced applications and development. The controlling methods include changing morphology, valence state, or surface modification, and introducing terminators (sulfuric acid, etc.) (He et al., 2021; Zandieh and Liu, 2023). Recently, various photoresponsive oxidases based on environmentally-friendly light stimulation have been developed. They do not require H2O2 or terminators, and can start or stop the oxidation process by turning on or off the light source. Additionally, the reaction systems are simpler, stable and controllable. However, in practical applications, photoresponsive oxidases often require illumination for 10–45 min, and the color development is shallow, which limits their detection ranges (Bhattacharyya et al., 2020; Duan et al., 2024; Li et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2019). Achieving higher catalytic efficiency to shorten the illumination time has become one key goal in the development of new photoresponsive oxidases.

Carbon dots (CDs) have become emerging photocatalysts in many fields (e.g., biochemical sensing) owing to their tunable optoelectronic properties, easy preparation, low costs, and eco-friendliness. However, relevant research focuses on fluorescent (FL) CDs, but rarely on room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) CDs. The efficient intersystem crossing (ISC) ability of RTP CDs enables them to have high excited triplet state (T1) quantum yield and long T1 exciton lifetime (Zhang et al., 2018). Moreover, dissolved oxygen can easily quench T1 through photochemical and photophysical pathways to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS). The long life can extend the effective interaction time of the collision between the two substances and helps increase the 1O2 quantum yield. Therefore, RTP CDs can be ideal photosensitive oxygen activators. Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) proposed by Yang et al., in 2018 are an important member of CDs. They have both the photoelectric properties of quantum dots and the advantages of polymer structures. Some CPDs exhibit dual properties of RTP and solid-state FL (Tao et al., 2018; Xia et al., 2024). However, relevant research focuses on the formation mechanism and optical property applications, but rarely on enzymatic properties. Therefore, the construction of a photosensitive analysis method based on RTP CPDs (PCPDs) is significant for broadening the detection methods of nanozymes.

Uric acid (UA), a product of purine metabolism, has antioxidant activity and plays an important role in maintaining normal physiological activities. Its abnormal levels are closely related to various diseases, such as hyperuricemia, gout, uremia and neurodegenerative diseases, so measurement of UA is crucial for the diagnosis, treatment and prevention of related diseases (Cheng et al., 2022). The UA content in serum is usually detected clinically, and is normally 100–420 μM. However, studies show the UA contents in urine and saliva of healthy people are 1.4–4.4 mM and 30–180 μM, respectively, and are significantly related to the UA content in serum (Chen et al., 2024; Sharma et al., 2024). Non-invasive sample collection featured by comfort has gradually become an ideal method in diagnosis and treatment. Currently, methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography, capillary electrophoresis, electrochemistry and colorimetry have been used in UA analysis (Cooper et al., 2006; Liang et al., 2021; Moravčík et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2021). Among them, colorimetric methods are popular because of low costs, easy operation and fast analysis, but mostly involve the UA decomposition by uricase to generate allantoin and H2O2, and then the combination with POD to catalyze the color reaction (Chen et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2022). Uricase is costly and fragile, and the existing enzyme-free colorimetric methods often have higher detection limits for UA (Liu et al., 2023; Qin et al., 2021). Therefore, developing sensitive, rapid and economical enzyme-free UA detection methods remains challenging.

This work is the first to study the enzymatic properties of PCPDs synthesized using polyacrylic acid (PAA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) as precursors. The Michaelis-Menten constant (K m) and maximum reaction velocity (V max) can be controlled, and low K m and extremely high V max can be achieved by optimizing the precursor ratio and preparation temperature. The catalytic chromogenic system was studied using RTP, FL, UV–visible absorption spectroscopy, free radical scavengers and electron paramagnetic resonance, and possible reaction mechanisms were proposed. Namely, the PCPDs have wide temperature adaptable ultra-efficient light-responsive oxidase-like activity, which can photosensitize dissolved oxygen into 1O2, ·O2 − and ·OH, and then strongly oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to a blue product. On this basis, a colorimetric-FL dual-mode method was constructed and combined with a smartphone to achieve UA analysis in serum, urine and saliva (Scheme 1 ). This study not only provides an economical, simple, sensitive and intuitive enzyme-free detection method for UA, but also offers a new type of highly efficient photoresponsive oxidases in the fields of analysis and sensing.
The detailed procedure was provided in Supplementary Information (SI).

The morphology and structure of carbonized polymer dots (PCPDs, VEDA = 50 μL, 180 °C) were characterized. The PCPDs are well-dispersed and nearly spherical nanoparticles in average size of about 6.63 nm (Fig. 1 A, S1A). HRTEM (Fig. 1B and C) shows the characteristic lattices of graphitic carbon (crystal plane 100, spacing 0.21 nm; crystal plane 002, spacing 0.34 nm). Other non-characteristic lattices (0.19 and 0.23 nm) may originate from the regular arrangement of polymer chains or slight carbonization (Tao et al., 2017). Most PCPDs have no lattice fringes, indicating the polymer properties are retained and are not completely carbonized at high temperatures. The broad diffraction peak at 22° in the XRD patterns (Fig. 1D) further confirms that PCPDs are mostly amorphous structures. The zeta potential is −8.8 mV (in ultrapure water, Fig. S1B), indicating the surface is negatively charged, which may be related to the rich carboxyl groups of the precursor PAA.

The functional group structure of PCPDs was further studied with FT-IR and XPS. The peak at 1444 cm−1 reflects the stretching vibration of N=O/C-O, and the peaks at 3251, 3059 and 2928 cm−1 indicate the stretching vibrations of N-H, O-H and C-H respectively. The C=O stretching vibration at 1629 cm−1, the N-H bending vibration at 1530 cm−1 and the C-N bending vibration at 1390 cm−1 correspond to the bands I, II and III of the amide respectively, indicating the existence of amide bonds in PCPDs (Fig. 1E). The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 1F) shows the main peaks are C 1s (284.8 eV), N 1s (399.8 eV) and O 1s (531.5 eV), indicating PCPDs are mainly composed of three elements: C, N and O. The C 1s fine spectrum peak is fitted to C-C/C=C (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286.1 eV), C=O (287.8 eV) and COOH (288.8 eV) (Fig. 1G). The N 1s fine spectrum is decomposed into C-N (399.7 eV) and N-H (401.3 eV) (Fig. 1H). The O 1s fine spectrum is converted into C=O (531.4 eV) and C-O (533.3 eV) (Fig. 1I). Both FT-IR and XPS show the surface of PCPDs is rich in oxygen and nitrogen containing groups, giving PCPDs excellent water solubility and efficient intermolecular hydrogen bonding, which is conducive to protonation and deprotonation (Gao et al., 2019).

PCPDs (VEDA = 50 μL, 180 °C) show a characteristic absorption peak at 336 nm (Fig. S2A), which is attributed to the n-π∗ transition of C=O/C=N. The liquid FL excitation and emission peaks are located at 330 and 398 nm respectively, the solid-state FL excitation and emission peaks are 346 and 421 nm respectively, and the RTP excitation and emission peaks are 360 (with a 340 nm shoulder) and 510 nm respectively. The high consistency between the excitation peaks and the characteristic absorption peaks indicates the photoluminescence mainly originates from C=O/C=N. The strong spin-orbit coupling of C=O/C=N can effectively fill triplet states through ISC (Liang et al., 2022). The FL lifetime (double-exponential fitting) and absolute FL quantum yield of the PCPDs are 7.04 ns and 26.8 %, respectively (Figs. S2B and C).

We developed the enzymatic properties of PCPDs for the first time, and characterized the optical properties under different preparation conditions to explore the potential relationship between the enzymatic properties and optical properties. Different PCPDs were prepared by keeping the mass of PAA constant and changing the EDA volume and hydrothermal temperature. The upper part of Fig. S2D shows as the volume of EDA increases (0–1000 μL), the absorption peak of PCPDs red-shifts from 330 to 336 nm and then blue-shifts to about 333 nm. The absorption intensity generally first rises to a maximum at the volume of 50 μL, and then falls until stabilizing. The color of the powder changes as light brown-warm white-yellowish. The PCPDs solutions with EDA volumes of 10 μL and 25 μL are turbid and require filtration before use, making quantification difficult. The lower part of Fig. S2D shows as the temperature rises (140–220 °C), the absorption peak of PCPDs (VEDA = 50 μL) red-shifts from 333 to about 336 nm. The absorption intensity generally rises first and then levels off at 180 °C as an inflection point. The color of the powder gradually becomes darker, and the yield is extremely low at above 240 °C, making it unsuitable for characterization and application. All batches of PCPDs have both excellent solid-state FL and RTP (Fig. 1J–L). As the volume of EDA increases, the afterglow turns from blue-green to green, and the RTP lifetime (three-exponential fitting) is generally prolonged. At the volume of 1000 μL, the afterglow up to 20 s can be observed with the naked eye, and the lifetime is 1.12 s. As the temperature increases, the RTP lifetime of PCPDs (VEDA = 50 μL) is first shortened and then extended, with 180 °C as the inflection point and a lifetime of 0.28 s.

As shown in Fig. S3A, The PCPDs mainly have two RTP centers, with the optimal excitation wavelengths at about 320 nm (center 1) and 360 nm (center 2), and the optimal emission wavelengths are both about 495 nm. As the EDA volume increases, RTP center 1 gradually dominates. The solid-liquid dual-phase FL of PCPDs shows similar excitation dependence, optimal excitation and emission wavelengths, but the solid-state FL half-width peak is wider, which may be caused by the peak broadening and tailing effects due to RTP. As the volume of EDA increases, the solid FL is first strengthened and then weakened, while the liquid FL is continually weakened (the liquid FL of PCPDs produced without EDA is extremely weak and can be ignored). As the temperature rises, the proportion of RTP center 1 decreases, the proportion of RTP center 3 (optimal excitation wavelength at about 430 nm) increases, and the liquid FL is continually increased (Fig. S3B).

TMB was used as a chromogenic substrate to evaluate the enzyme-like activity of PCPDs. The PCPDs can strongly oxidize colorless TMB into blue ox-TMB (Abs: 652 nm) under 365 nm ultraviolet light, and the absorbance is far larger than that of the light-free and PCPDs-free systems (Fig. 2 A), indicating the PCPDs have photoinduced oxidase-like activity. To achieve the best catalytic effect, we also systematically optimized pH, PCPD concentration, TMB concentration, temperature and illumination time. Based on comprehensive consideration of catalytic ability and operational convenience, the optimal reaction conditions were determined to be pH 3.4, 1 mg mL−1 PCPDs, 1 mM TMB, room temperature, and UV irradiation at 365 nm for 60 s (Fig. 2B–F). The PCPDs showed cold-resistant enzyme-like activity at −20 °C (fully frozen state). The catalytic activity of PCPDs remained high after 3 months of storage (Fig. 2G). In addition, the TMB oxidation reaction showed a staircase-like pattern change with the turn-on or turn-off of the UV light source, confirming the light-responsive oxidase-like activity of PCPDs and enabling accurate control of the catalytic process (Fig. 2H).

We also analyzed the steady-state kinetics of PCPDs under different preparation conditions to further understand their photocontrolled oxidase activity. Figs. S4A–O shows the time kinetic curves of PCPDs at different TMB concentrations. On this basis, the classic Michaelis-Menten curves (Figs. S5A and B) and Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plots (Figs. S5C and D) were obtained, and K m and V max were computed. The K m of PCPDs prepared at 140–220 °C first decreases and then stabilizes, and V max first increases and then stabilizes (Fig. S5F). Thus, the optimal preparation temperature was determined to be 180 °C. The PCPDs without EDA have a maximum K m (0.8344 mM) and minimum V max (0.3886 × 10−6 M s−1) (Fig. S5E). The PCPDs containing 50 μL of EDA have a lower K m (0.1266 mM) and maximum V max (2.0194 × 10−6 M s−1); K m decreases by about 6.6 times, and V max increases by about 5.2 times. The low K m is due to the high affinity between the hydrophobic region of PCPDs and TMB (Xia et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2018), which is further confirmed by the low K m of the low-water-soluble PCPDs prepared with 10 or 25 μL of EDA. Moreover, the trend of V max is roughly the same as the absorption intensity trend in Fig. S2D, indicating the n-π∗ transition of C=O/C=N is closely related to the catalytic activity. Compared with most natural enzymes and nanozymes (Table S1), PCPDs have lower K m and extremely high V max, suggesting the PCPDs have extremely high affinity and catalytic activity for TMB, which can significantly accelerate reactions in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism on photoresponsive oxidase-like activity of PCPDs
ce:section.ce:para: A series of verification experiments were conducted to explore the catalytic mechanism of PCPDs. Ventilation with N2 for 10 min can significantly reduce the absorbance of the solution, indicating dissolved oxygen is a key precursor for the generation of ROS (Fig. 3 A). The ROS species were further determined through scavenging experiments. CAT, EtOH, mannitol, SOD and Try were used as scavengers of H2O2, h+, ·OH, ·O2 −and 1O2 respectively. Try, SOD and mannitol significantly inhibited TMB oxidation, indicating 1O2, ·O2 − and ·OH are the main ROS as-generated (Fig. 3B). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was used to further identify ROS species. The 1:1:1, 1:1:1:1 and 1:2:2:1 characteristic signal under UV light confirm the generation of 1O2, ·O2 − and ·OH, while there is no relevant signal under dark conditions (Fig. 3C–E). Based on the above results, the catalytic mechanism of PCPDs is shown below (Fig. 3F). After PCPDs absorb photon energy under ultraviolet light, the valence electrons transit from the ground state (S0) to the excited singlet state (Sn), and the molecules in the first excited singlet state (S1) are radiatively deexcited to emit FL and return to S0. In addition, electrons in S1 can reach the first excited triplet state (T1) through ISC. T1 has a long life and transits back to S0, emitting RTP. At the same time, T1 of PCPDs can transfer electrons to adjacent triplet molecules (mainly 3O2) to form ·O2 − and ·OH (type I photochemical reaction), and can transfer energy to 3O2 through collision to form 1O2 (type II photochemical reaction)(Zhang et al., 2018). Finally, various photo-driven ROS rapidly oxidize colorless TMB into blue ox-TMB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and mechanism of uric acid detection
ce:section.ce:para: It was previously confirmed that PCPDs have excellent light-responsive oxidase-like activity and can quickly oxidize TMB and turn it blue under ultraviolet light. After the addition of UA, the blue of the solution fades, indicating the system has the potential for colorimetric detection of UA (Fig. S6A). Moreover, the solvent in which UA is dissolved greatly affects the analytical performance (Fig. S6B). With pH 7.4 PB as the solvent, the absorbance decreases and FL is enhanced the most obviously, and the sensitivity is significantly enhanced. However, when water or pH 7.4 Tris-HCl buffer is used as the solvent, the performance is extremely poor. The reason for these results is that UA can form more soluble urate in pH 7.4 PB, and complete dissolution contributes to full reaction (Iwata et al., 1989). The reaction time at 50 °C is only 2 min, compared with 40 min and hours at 37 and 25 °C, respectively (Fig. S6C). Finally, 50 °C was determined to be the optimal temperature for UA reaction.

The mechanism of PCPDs detecting UA is shown in Fig. 3G. In brief, PCPDs photosensitize dissolved oxygen under 365 nm UV light to 1O2, ·O2 − and ·OH, and abundant ROS quickly oxidize TMB into blue ox-TMB. The newly-added UA can remove ROS and acts as a reducing agent to reduce ox-TMB to colorless TMB, achieving colorimetric detection of UA. At the same time, ox-TMB quenches the FL of the system through the internal filter effect, and the FL is recovered after addition of UA, achieving the FL detection of UA.

The effect of UA concentration on the TMB-PCPDs photosensitive chromogenic system was studied under the optimal conditions. Increasing UA concentration can decrease the absorbance of ox-TMB at 652 nm and intensify the FL of the system (Fig. 4 A and B). The blue to colorless transition and the enhanced FL of the solution are visible with the naked eye. The absorbance well linearly decreases with the increase in UA concentration (0.6–55 μM) (Fig. 4C). The regression equation is A = −0.0315 [UA] + 1.7602 (R2 = 0.9988), and the limit of detection (LOD, 3σ/S) is 77.8 nM. FL intensity well linearly rises with the increase of UA concentration (0.6–60 μM). The regression equation is F = 9.3649 [UA] + 350.15 (R2 = 0.9969), and the LOD (3σ/S) is 48.1 nM. Based on the obvious chromatic changes of the sensing system, a smartphone and the open-source software “Color Grab” were used to read the RGB values of the solution under normal light or UV. Moreover, a 4 × 4 colorimetric array was drawn (Fig. 4D–F). The gray value and UA concentration are well linearly related (Gray = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B) (Fig. 4E–G), and the LOD (3σ/S) are 0.41 and 0.13 μM respectively. The dual-mode spectroscopy has higher sensitivity and wider linear range in detecting UA, and outperforms most existing detection methods (Table S2). The smartphone colorimetric analysis method also performs well and does not rely on professional testing equipment. We can also semi-quantify UA by visually observing the solution color combined with the array pattern.

To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of this method for UA detection, the effects of various common compounds and ions in organisms on the detection system were studied. UA caused a decrease in absorbance and an increase in FL. High-concentration (400 μM) leucine (Leu), phenylalanine (Phe), tryptophan (Trp), valine (Val), glucose (Glu) and urea, high-concentration (800 μM) Al3+, Ca2+, NO3 −, Cu2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, NH4 + and Zn2+, 2 μM cysteine (Cys), ascorbic acid (AA) and glutathione (GSH), 4.8 μM Fe2+ and 48 μM Fe3+ led to negligible effects on the dual-mode sensing system (Fig. 4H and I). Cys, AA, GSH and Fe2+ have strong reducing properties, and at high concentrations, will seriously affect sensing. However, their concentrations in human serum, urine and saliva are much lower than the UA level, and their effects can be ignored after appropriate dilution (Fang et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2023; Ngamchuea et al., 2018). Selectivity, competitiveness and potential ion interference experiments show this method is highly specific for UA and has strong resistance against potential ion interference, making it suitable for sensing UA in complex biological samples.

To verify the reliability of this method in practical applications, spike recovery experiments were performed in serum, urine and saliva. All three matrices were diluted to make the UA concentration fall within the linear range of this method. The spike recovery rates of UA in serum, urine and saliva are 93.24–103.59 %, 92.93–101.36 % and 93.93–108.01 respectively, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) are less than 4.71 %, 5.63 % and 7.13 % respectively (Table S3). The recovery rate and RSD of UA by the smartphone RGB method are slightly inferior to those of the spectroscopic method, but still meet the actual analysis needs. The results were relatively consistent compared with a commercial kit. The above suggest the UA analysis method as-developed has high accuracy and precision, and can be used to effectively detect UA in serum, urine and saliva.

This study reports for the first time that PCPDs prepared from PAA and EDA as precursors have photoresponsive oxidase-like activity, and can efficiently catalyze the coloration of TMB. The catalytic performance of PCPDs towards TMB was improved several times by optimizing the preparation conditions. Uric acid (UA) is a common free radical scavenger and has strong reducing properties, which can inhibit the coloration of the sensing platform and cause fluorescence (FL) changes. On this basis, a colorimetric/FL dual-mode method based on PCPDs was constructed for detecting UA in human serum, urine and saliva. This method has high sensitivity, wide linear range, low cost and fast speed. Moreover, the dual-mode RGB value by combining with smartphone-based sample recognition can be used to quantify UA through gray value calculation. Depending on the actual situations, single or multiple models can be used to support each other and improve the analysis accuracy. This sensing platform does not require H2O2, biological enzymes, reaction terminators or sulfhydryl scavengers. In summary, PCPDs as excellent photoresponsive oxidases are not only suitable for detecting UA in complex biological samples, but may also show broad application prospects in nanozyme-based analysis and forgery prevention.

Shiqing Luo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiaojie Sun: Validation, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lifang Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Yanming Miao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Guiqin Yan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) (32171393, 31700862), the Shanxi Province Postgraduate Education Innovation Project (2020BY083), and the Graduate Science and Technology Innovation Project of Shanxi Normal University (2019XBY014).
